senses.definition: Made friendly again after a disagreement; reconciled (now very rare or obsolete).
examples: The two families, long divided by quarrels, were at last affrended.
Once affrended, the former rivals worked together in peace.

senses.definition: A fierce attack or violent onset (obsolete).
examples: The castle walls shook under the sudden affret of the enemy.
With loud cries they made an affret upon the gate.

senses.definition: Plural of affret: fierce attacks or violent onsets (obsolete).
examples: The city had endured many affrets over the centuries.
Their chronicles tell of the affrets that broke the old kingdom.

senses.definition: In music, directing the player to speed up with a sense of urgency.
examples: The score marks affrettando just before the climax.
She played the final bars affrettando to build excitement.

senses.definition: Very ugly, unpleasant, or horrible (a French word sometimes used in English style or criticism).
examples: The décor was deliberately affreux, almost painful to look at.
He called the painting "absolutely affreux" in his review.

senses.definition: A speech sound that begins as a stop and then is released as a fricative, like the sound at the start of "chair".
examples: In English, the sounds at the start of "church" and "judge" are affricates.
Learners often confuse an affricate with a simple fricative.

senses.definition: Pronounced as or changed into an affricate sound.
examples: In some accents, this stop is slightly affricated.
The consonant became affricated in later stages of the language.

senses.definition: Plural of affricate: speech sounds that start as stops and end as fricatives.
examples: English has two common affricates, as in "church" and "judge".
The chart lists fricatives, affricates, and plosives.

senses.definition: Changing a sound so that it is or becomes an affricate.
examples: The sound change is affricating an earlier stop.
The dialect is gradually affricating this consonant cluster.

senses.definition: The process in which a sound becomes an affricate.
examples: Affrication commonly occurs before high front vowels.
The historical record shows a stage of affrication in this language.

senses.definition: Plural of affrication: different cases or types of sounds becoming affricates.
examples: The study compares several affrications across related dialects.
These affrications are conditioned by following vowels.

senses.definition: Relating to or having the quality of an affricate sound.
examples: The language has several affricative consonants.
An affricative release can be heard before the vowel.

senses.definition: Affricate consonants or sounds with affricate-like qualities.
examples: The inventory of affricatives varies widely between languages.
These affricatives contrast with simple fricatives in the system.

senses.definition: Made friends; brought into a friendly relationship (obsolete).
examples: Once enemies, they were now affriended and at peace.
The kingdoms stood affriended after the treaty.

senses.definition: To frighten someone greatly or suddenly (now rare or literary).
examples: The sudden scream did much to affright the children.
Strange tales were told to affright them from wandering at night.

senses.definition: Sudden or great fear; a state of being very frightened (archaic).
examples: He woke in an affright, trembling in the dark.
The sight of the storm filled them with affright.

senses.definition: Very frightened or filled with sudden fear (literary).
examples: The affrighted child clung to her mother.
Affrighted villagers fled from the burning town.

senses.definition: In a very frightened or terrified way (literary).
examples: She stared affrightedly at the dark figure.
The horses snorted and stepped back affrightedly.

senses.definition: To terrify or frighten greatly (obsolete or dialect).
examples: The thunder did much to affrighten the small child.
They used ghost stories to affrighten the newcomers.

senses.definition: Very frightened or alarmed (obsolete or dialect).
examples: The affrightened servants hid in the cellar.
He stood affrightened at the sight before him.

senses.definition: Causing great fear or sudden terror (rare or literary).
examples: It was an affrightening spectacle in the dead of night.
They told affrightening stories of the haunted woods.

senses.definition: Third person singular of affrighten: frightens or terrifies (obsolete or dialect).
examples: The strange noise affrightens the dog every night.
Her harsh tone affrightens the younger students.

senses.definition: Someone or something that causes great fear (rare or literary).
examples: The storm was an affrighter of sailors for generations.
He became the affrighter of timid children with his ghost stories.

senses.definition: Very frightening or terrifying (obsolete or humorous).
examples: They told an affrightful tale of ghosts and curses.
The noise was affrightful in the narrow alley.

senses.definition: In a very frightening or terrifying way (obsolete or humorous).
examples: The wind howled affrightfully around the tower.
She screamed affrightfully at the sudden shadow.

senses.definition: Present participle of affright: frightening strongly (literary).
examples: The thunder was affrighting the farm animals.
Grim stories were affrighting the children in the dark room.

senses.definition: In a way that causes great fear or terror (literary).
examples: The room was affrightingly silent.
It was affrightingly easy to get lost in the mist.

senses.definition: The act of frightening, or the state of being greatly frightened (archaic).
examples: The sudden affrightment left him speechless.
In their affrightment they forgot all orders.

senses.definition: Plural of affrightment: incidents or states of strong sudden fear (archaic).
examples: His childhood was full of affrightments and hardships.
The tale is a string of affrightments and escapes.

senses.definition: Third person singular of affright: frightens or terrifies (literary).
examples: The thunder affrights the cattle every stormy night.
Such a thought affrights him more than death.

senses.definition: Plural of affright: instances of sudden or great fear (archaic).
examples: The story is filled with affrights and narrow escapes.
Childhood affrights often stay in the memory.

senses.definition: A remark or action that deliberately insults or offends someone.
examples: The decision was seen as an affront to local residents.
He took her silence as a personal affront.

senses.definition: To insult or offend someone, especially by not showing respect.
examples: She was deeply affronted by his rude behaviour.
The comments affronted many members of the community.

senses.definition: In heraldry, shown facing forward towards the viewer.
examples: The shield bears a lion affronte at its centre.
They chose an eagle affronte as their emblem.

senses.definition: Feeling angry or offended because of what someone has said or done.
examples: He looked genuinely affronted by the suggestion.
She felt affronted when they ignored her advice.

senses.definition: In a way that shows you feel insulted or offended.
examples: She turned away affrontedly and refused to answer.
He spoke affrontedly, as if he had been unfairly attacked.

senses.definition: The feeling or state of being offended or insulted.
examples: Her affrontedness was obvious from her tight smile.
He hid his affrontedness behind a joking remark.

senses.definition: A person who is insulted or offended by someone’s words or actions.
examples: The affrontee demanded a public apology.
As the affrontee, she chose not to respond in anger.

senses.definition: Someone who insults or deliberately offends another person.
examples: The affronter showed no sign of regret.
In this dispute, he was clearly the affronter, not the victim.

senses.definition: Deliberately insulting or offensive.
examples: They made several affronting remarks about his background.
Her affronting attitude angered the whole team.

senses.definition: Present participle of affront: insulting or offending someone.
examples: By ignoring their concerns, the company was affronting its customers.
He kept affronting colleagues with careless jokes.

senses.definition: In a way that is openly insulting or offensive.
examples: He spoke affrontingly about their traditions.
The article affrontingly mocked the victims.

senses.definition: The quality of being openly insulting or offensive.
examples: The affrontingness of his remarks shocked the audience.
She complained about the sheer affrontingness of the ad campaign.

senses.definition: Acts or instances of insulting or offending someone (rare).
examples: He apologised for his earlier affrontings.
The history of the conflict is full of mutual affrontings.

senses.definition: Likely to insult or offend; having an insulting quality.
examples: They considered the proposal affrontive to their rights.
His affrontive language damaged negotiations.

senses.definition: The tendency or quality of causing offence or insult.
examples: Critics noted the affrontiveness of the campaign slogan.
The film’s affrontiveness was part of its appeal to some viewers.

senses.definition: The act of insulting or offending someone, or the state of feeling insulted (rare).
examples: The comment was a clear affrontment of her honour.
He spoke in a tone of affrontment after being excluded.

senses.definition: Plural of affront: remarks or actions that deliberately insult or offend people.
examples: They saw the new law as one of many affronts to their freedoms.
Over the years he had suffered several affronts to his pride.

senses.definition: Third person singular of affront: insults or offends someone.
examples: This policy affronts many local residents.
The headline affronts everyone who cares about fairness.

senses.definition: Having an insolent or rudely bold manner (very rare, humorous or dialect).
examples: He gave me an affronty grin and walked away.
Teenagers can be strangely affronty when challenged.

senses.definition: A short form sometimes used in legal or bureaucratic writing for "affidavit" or a related term (very rare).
examples: The clerk filed the afft with the court archive.
Please attach the signed afft to your application.

senses.definition: To pour a liquid, especially water, over something or someone (formal or technical).
examples: The priest would affuse water over the child’s head.
They affused the surface with a gentle stream of water.

senses.definition: A likely error or misprint combining "affused" and "affusing"; not a standard word.
examples: The form "affusedaffusing" appears to be a typographical error.
He corrected "affusedaffusing" to "affused or affusing" in the text.

senses.definition: The act of pouring a liquid on something, especially the pouring of water over a person in a religious ceremony instead of full immersion.
examples: The church practises baptism by affusion rather than by immersion.
Affusion was chosen because the river was frozen.

senses.definition: Plural of affusion: acts or instances of pouring a liquid on something, especially in religious or medical contexts.
examples: The ritual involved several affusions with scented water.
Ancient texts describe various affusions used in healing.
